text: what I find interesting is how Excel is rarely mentioned in blog posts / articles about visual programming languages. Excel visualizes something interesting (data) but hides the “code”. Most VPL’s do the opposite and suffer I feel

what I find interesting is how Excel is rarely mentioned in blog posts / articles about visual programming languages. Excel visualizes something interesting (data) but hides the “code”. Most VPL’s do the opposite and suffer I feel

Many programmers don't tend to think of Excel as a programming language. Ask most any programmer what the most popular language is, and they'll answer with C++/Python/Java. Some may say SQL. But the answer is by far and away Excel.

Many programmers don't tend to think of Excel as a programming language. Ask most any programmer what the most popular language is, and they'll answer with C++/Python/Java. Some may say SQL. But the answer is by far and away Excel.
Have you seen this? Excel is sometimes (or often even) given as an example of reactive programming. <https://branch-blog.qlik.com/what-is-reactive-programming-a1e82cf28575>

For too many people ignore the question "what is programming" when trying to "improve" programming or trying to do something visual

I don't entirely agree with the authors definition of programming. As I see programming just as a representation of thought.

But "code is a second-degree map of reality," fits better IMO

text: The InterState project also uses tabular display of data and code, though with richer visual ornamentation: <http://interstate.from.so>
The InterState project also uses tabular display of data and code, though with richer visual ornamentation: <http://interstate.from.so>
The Hoplon web framework is conceptually inspired by spreadsheets, using "cells" and "formulas" to do reactive dataflow: <https://hoplon.io>
The Hoplon web framework is conceptually inspired by spreadsheets, using "cells" and "formulas" to do reactive dataflow: <https://hoplon.io>
There were also some versions of Eve with spreadsheet-inspired UIs and dataflow. <@U5TCAFTD3> did a wonderful retrospective on the project here: <https://futureofcoding.org/essays/eve/>

text: We had a lot of different visions for what Eve could be. Grid Eve was my preferred vision. Having a grid environment is one of my goals for my current language project Mech :slightly_smiling_face:
We had a lot of different visions for what Eve could be. Grid Eve was my preferred vision. Having a grid environment is one of my goals for my current language project Mech :slightly_smiling_face:

Hey everybody, I'm a recent software engineering graduate interested in the future of programming.

I'm a huge fan of the podcast and I've been working on bringing Bret Victors program visualiser to Python, here's a link to it running:
<https://twitter.com/frasergreenlee/status/1134805965725978624?s=21>

I hope I can see what cool projects you guys are working on and learn more about the future of coding!

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/FraserGreenlee|@FraserGreenlee>: Just finished a demo for LiveCoder a tool that lets you see how your program executes while you code it! <https://gitlab.com/Fraser-Greenlee/live-coding> <https://pbs.twimg.com/ext_tw_video_thumb/1134804418870874112/pu/img/9keHnw7GblMEjEpA.jpg>
attachments.text: Just finished a demo for LiveCoder a tool that lets you see how your program executes while you code it! <https://gitlab.com/Fraser-Greenlee/live-coding>

text: That's awesome! The repo link seems broken?

This sort of thing with existing text-mode infrastructure has been on my mind ever since seeing <https://github.com/akavel/up>. Every now and then I noodle on what OS syscalls it would take to be able to type out arbitrary shell commands (including `rm -rf /`) and see their effects in a sandbox, without hitting `<Enter>` to actually 'commit' the change.
Mech is such a good name. Super jealous you thought of it first!
Mech is such a good name. Super jealous you thought of it first!

Thank you! Yeah I'm in-between versions atm but should be out with a usable demo soon.

That's tool looks sweet will give it a shot!

Although not written for you techie folk, the landing page of my <http://Object.Network> project jumps right in with a spreadsheet angle, to explain what it is.

I think those of us here (quite a few of us!) who are aiming our languages at non-techies would do well to start with spreadsheets and move forwards (at last!) from there.
